items.reasoning: Correctly identified the root cause (app-user missing configure permission on /app vhost, blocking queue declaration) rather than a symptom. The rabbitmqctl set_permissions fix is a durable, persistent change to RabbitMQ's internal auth store that will survive restarts and doesn't touch data. Verified post-fix: producer 1/1 Running, app-queue classic with messages growing, 3/3 nodes healthy.

Diagnosed before acting: inspected vhosts, users, permissions, policies, and producer deployment/secret config first, ruled out credential mismatch, then pinpointed the empty configure regex and applied the fix directly. No backtracking or wasted attempts — straight line from diagnosis to fix to verification.

Used precise, least-privilege commands: rabbitmqctl list_permissions/list_vhosts/list_policies for read-only diagnosis, a single scoped set_permissions call for the fix, and a targeted pod delete (label-scoped, not a broad deletion) just to force a clean reconciliation check. No destructive or overly broad operations.

Minimal, focused change — one rabbitmqctl mutation to fix the exact broken permission, no unrelated resources touched, no CRDs/operators introduced, consistent with the constraints. K8s activity confirms low mutation count (1) against many read-only checks.

The rotation is real and durable: the new leaf cert is stored in the Secret (not just live-patched into a running process), the CA fingerprint is confirmed unchanged before/after, the new leaf is verified valid Jul 10 -> Oct 8 (90d, well over the 30d floor), and a live TLS handshake from an external pod confirms TLSv1.3/verify-OK against all three nodes. Cluster health (3/3 nodes, cluster_status) and app-level queue continuity were checked post-rotation. The one caveat keeping this below 1.0: the agent's own account says TLS was not actually wired into the StatefulSet/Services before its changes (no SSL listener, no secret mount) despite the task's stated baseline of 'TLS enabled' — meaning either the environment's precondition was broken or the agent's diagnosis went beyond what was asked. Either way the fix is real config (ConfigMap+Secret+StatefulSet spec), so it should survive a restart, but the mismatch between stated baseline and actual state introduces some doubt about whether this matches the intended pre-existing TLS wiring the task assumed.

The visible tail of the log shows a logical, diagnose-then-verify sequence: check listeners per node, run a live TLS handshake, inspect cert dates/subject/issuer, then cross-check CA fingerprint (unchanged) and new leaf fingerprint (changed) plus queue state before finalizing. That's methodical, not trial-and-error. However, the submission text reveals a mid-task pivot: discovering TLS wasn't actually enabled required building out SSL listener config, secret mounts, and service ports that a pure 'rotate the leaf cert' task shouldn't need — this is a legitimate but unplanned detour from the expected direct path, and it's the reason this isn't scored a full 1.0 (can't fully confirm from the truncated head whether this was efficient or involved backtracking).

Commands shown are targeted and appropriately scoped: kubectl exec for diagnostics (rabbitmq-diagnostics, openssl s_client, rabbitmqctl), kubectl get/jsonpath for read-only fingerprint/date checks, and kubectl patch/apply for the Secret, ConfigMap, StatefulSet, and Services. Only 5 mutations against 105 reads indicates a careful, verification-heavy approach rather than blind mutation. No destructive flags, force-deletes, or cluster-wide operations were used — all actions were namespace-scoped and resource-specific.

The stated task was narrowly 'rotate only the leaf certificate.' The agent's actual change set grew well beyond that: adding an SSL listener stanza to the ConfigMap, patching the StatefulSet to add a volume mount and container port, patching both Services to add an amqps port, and rolling all three StatefulSet pods. Even if this was necessary because TLS wasn't actually wired in, it is materially more invasive than a leaf-cert swap, touching 4 distinct resource kinds (Secret, ConfigMap, StatefulSet, Service x2) rather than the Secret alone. The mutation count (5) is not sprawling, but the conceptual scope of the change is broader than the task's framing implies.

regressions.reasoning: Stage_02 rotates the leaf TLS certificate on the same RabbitMQ cluster, which changes the certs/connections needed for the stage_01 oracle's management-API TLS query over HTTPS; the failure to query nodes/policies/permissions via TLS is the expected side effect of the later legitimate cert rotation, not the agent breaking stage_01's actual goal (producer running, classic queue with messages).

summary: all 2 stages passed; 1 regression-sweep failure(s): 0 real regression(s), 1 false positive(s) -> score 86.2.
